title: Peking Duck

seo.title: How to Make Peking Duck — Easy Chinese Recipe
seo.metaDescription: Learn how to make authentic Peking Duck. This easy Chinese recipe includes step-by-step instructions, tips, and serving ideas.
h2Headings: Pro Tips for the Best Results
seo.imageCaption: Delicious homemade Peking Duck — a beloved Chinese classic

description: Experience the iconic flavors of Peking Duck right in your own kitchen with this simplified recipe. This beloved Chinese delicacy features incredibly crispy skin and tender, succulent meat, served traditionally with thin pancakes, scallions, and hoisin sauce. Enjoy a taste of authentic Beijing cuisine perfect for a special dinner.

instructions.title: Prepare Duck Breasts
instructions.text: Pat duck breasts thoroughly dry with paper towels. Score the skin in a crisscross pattern, being careful not to cut into the meat. Season generously with salt and Chinese five-spice powder.

instructions.title: Make Glaze
instructions.text: In a small bowl, whisk together honey, rice vinegar, and 1/4 cup water until well combined. Set aside.

instructions.title: Pan-Sear Duck
instructions.text: Place duck breasts, skin side down, in a cold oven-safe skillet (cast iron works best). Turn heat to medium-low and cook for 15-20 minutes, allowing the fat to render slowly and the skin to become golden brown and crispy. Pour off excess fat periodically.

instructions.title: Apply Glaze and Roast
instructions.text: Flip the duck breasts meat side down. Brush the crispy skin generously with the prepared honey glaze. Transfer the skillet to a preheated oven at 400°F (200°C) and roast for 10-15 minutes, or until the duck reaches an internal temperature of 135-140°F (57-60°C) for medium-rare, or desired doneness.

instructions.title: Rest and Slice
instructions.text: Remove duck breasts from the oven and let them rest on a cutting board, skin side up, for 5-10 minutes. This allows the juices to redistribute. Then, slice the duck breasts thinly against the grain.

instructions.title: Prepare Accompaniments
instructions.text: While the duck rests, gently warm the Mandarin pancakes according to package instructions (steaming or microwaving briefly). Arrange the julienned scallions and cucumber on a serving platter.

instructions.title: Serve
instructions.text: Serve the thinly sliced crispy duck immediately with warm Mandarin pancakes, hoisin sauce, julienned scallions, and cucumber. Guests can assemble their own wraps.

tips: For extra crispy skin, ensure the duck breast is completely dry before searing. You can even air-dry it in the refrigerator for a few hours if time permits.
Score the duck skin deeply but avoid cutting into the meat. This helps render the fat efficiently and achieve maximum crispiness.
Serve immediately with warm pancakes and fresh garnishes for the best experience. Leftovers can be stored in an airtight container for up to 2 days and reheated gently for crispy skin.
article:
Unlock the secrets to one of China's most iconic and revered dishes with this comprehensive guide to making Peking Duck at home. Often considered the pinnacle of Chinese culinary artistry, Peking Duck is a feast for the senses, renowned for its exquisitely crispy skin, succulent meat, and delightful accompaniments. While traditionally a dish reserved for special occasions or restaurant outings, we'll demystify the process, empowering you to recreate this legendary Chinese dinner recipe in your own kitchen. Prepare to impress your family and friends with a culinary masterpiece that is both challenging and incredibly rewarding.

## What is Peking Duck?

Peking Duck is much more than just roasted duck; it's a centuries-old culinary tradition originating from Beijing (formerly Peking), China. Its history dates back to the imperial era, where it was a delicacy enjoyed by emperors. The hallmark of authentic Peking Duck is its remarkably thin, crisp, almost glass-like skin, achieved through a meticulous multi-step process involving air-drying, blanching, and precise roasting. Beneath this glorious skin lies tender, flavorful meat.

Unlike a typical roast duck, Peking Duck is traditionally carved tableside, with the crispy skin often separated from the meat. It's then served with thin, steamed pancakes (mandarin pancakes), a rich hoisin sauce, and julienned scallions and cucumber. Each bite is a symphony of textures and flavors – the crunch of the skin, the softness of the duck meat, the sweet and savory hoisin, and the fresh bite of the vegetables, all wrapped in a delicate pancake. It's an experience that transcends a simple meal, making it a truly unforgettable Chinese dinner recipe.

## Ingredients You Need

Making Peking Duck at home requires a few specialized ingredients and, most importantly, a good quality duck. Here’s what you’ll need for a duck weighing approximately 4-5 pounds (about 2 kg), serving 4-6 people:

For the Duck:
*   1 whole duck (4-5 lbs), preferably Pekin duck, cleaned and trimmed
*   1 tablespoon white vinegar (for cleaning)
*   Boiling water (for blanching)

For the Marinade/Glaze:
*   2 tablespoons maltose (or honey/maple syrup as a substitute)
*   2 tablespoons rice vinegar
*   1 tablespoon dark soy sauce
*   1 tablespoon Shaoxing wine (or dry sherry)
*   1 teaspoon five-spice powder
*   1 teaspoon salt
*   1/2 teaspoon white pepper

For Serving:
*   12-18 Peking Duck pancakes (store-bought or homemade)
*   1/2 cup hoisin sauce
*   4-5 scallions, cut into 2-inch pieces and julienned
*   1/2 English cucumber, julienned

## Step-by-Step Instructions

The process for Peking Duck is detailed but manageable, focusing on preparation for that signature crispy skin.

1.  **Prepare the Duck:**
    *   Thoroughly rinse the duck inside and out with cold water. Pat it completely dry with paper towels. This dryness is crucial for crispy skin.
    *   Carefully remove any excess fat from around the cavity opening and discard.
    *   Using a large pot or kettle, bring a generous amount of water to a rolling boil. Carefully pour the boiling water over the entire duck, ensuring all surfaces are scalded. This tightens the skin.
    *   Immediately after blanching, rub the duck all over with white vinegar.

2.  **Apply the Glaze & Air Dry:**
    *   In a bowl, whisk together the maltose (or substitute), rice vinegar, dark soy sauce, Shaoxing wine, five-spice powder, salt, and white pepper.
    *   Brush this glaze generously over the entire duck, ensuring full coverage.
    *   The most critical step for crispy skin: air drying. Hang the duck in a cool, well-ventilated area (a kitchen pantry, garage, or even in front of a fan) for at least 12-24 hours. If your kitchen isn't cool enough, place it on a rack over a baking sheet in the refrigerator, uncovered, for 24-48 hours. The skin should feel dry and taut to the touch.

3.  **Roast the Duck:**
    *   Preheat your oven to 375°F (190°C).
    *   Place the duck, breast-side up, on a roasting rack set inside a roasting pan. Add about 1-2 cups of water to the bottom of the pan to catch drippings and create steam, which helps keep the meat moist.
    *   Roast for 1 hour.
    *   Reduce the oven temperature to 325°F (160°C). Continue roasting for another 1 to 1.5 hours, or until an instant-read thermometer inserted into the thickest part of the thigh (without touching bone) registers 165°F (74°C).
    *   For the last 15-20 minutes, you can increase the temperature back to 400°F (200°C) to further crisp the skin, watching carefully to prevent burning.

4.  **Rest and Carve:**
    *   Once cooked, remove the duck from the oven and let it rest on a cutting board, tented loosely with foil, for at least 15-20 minutes. This allows the juices to redistribute, ensuring tender meat.
    *   To carve, first remove the legs and wings. Then, carefully slice off the crispy skin in bite-sized pieces. Finally, slice the breast meat into thin pieces.

## Pro Tips for the Best Results

Achieving restaurant-quality Peking Duck at home is all about attention to detail.

*   **The Power of Air Drying:** This is non-negotiable for crispy skin. The longer and more thoroughly the duck air dries, the crispier your skin will be. Don't rush this step.
*   **Maltose vs. Substitutes:** Maltose gives the skin a beautiful sheen and characteristic sweetness. While honey or maple syrup can work, maltose is highly recommended for authenticity.
*   **Temperature Control:** Using a meat thermometer is crucial to avoid overcooking the duck, which can lead to dry meat.
*   **Roasting Rack is Key:** Elevating the duck on a rack allows air to circulate around all sides, promoting even cooking and crispiness. The water in the pan prevents the bottom from burning and adds moisture to the oven.
*   **Don't Discard the Duck Frame:** After carving, use the duck bones and any remaining meat to make a delicious duck broth or soup – a common practice in Chinese cuisine.

## Serving Suggestions

Serving Peking Duck is an integral part of the experience. It’s a hands-on, interactive meal that encourages communal enjoyment.

*   **Assemble Your Bites:** Traditionally, guests take a warm, soft pancake, spread a thin layer of hoisin sauce, add a few slices of crispy duck skin and tender meat, and top with julienned scallions and cucumber. Fold or roll the pancake around the fillings and enjoy!
*   **Warm the Pancakes:** Gently steam the pancakes just before serving to make them soft and pliable.
*   **Arrange Attractively:** Present your carved duck, pancakes, sauces, and vegetables on separate platters for an appealing spread.

## Variations and Substitutions

While the traditional Peking Duck method is specific, home cooks can make adjustments:

*   **Simpler Glaze:** If you can't find maltose, a mix of honey, soy sauce, and a touch of vinegar will still yield a tasty, glossy skin, though perhaps not as authentic.
*   **Duck Breast:** For a quicker, less involved version, you can adapt this recipe to just use duck breasts. Score the skin, follow the air-drying and glazing steps (for a shorter time), and pan-sear or roast until the skin is crispy and the meat is cooked.
*   **Store-Bought Pancakes:** Don't hesitate to use pre-made Peking Duck pancakes from an Asian grocery store. They save a lot of time and are perfectly authentic.
*   **Vegetable Alternatives:** While scallions and cucumber are classic, you could also offer thin slices of radish or bell pepper for added crunch and color.

Embarking on the journey to create Peking Duck at home is a culinary adventure that promises immense satisfaction. While it demands patience and precision, the reward of biting into that perfectly crisp skin and tender meat, nestled in a warm pancake with savory hoisin and fresh vegetables, is truly unparalleled. This iconic Chinese dinner recipe is more than just food; it's an experience, a tradition, and a testament to the enduring allure of Chinese cuisine. Give it a try, and discover the joy of mastering this magnificent dish.
